feature_description: Restore orphaned-Feature cleanup that was lost when we removed RemoveOrphanedFeatures from PreprocessDataBeforeSave in commit 994ac67b (fix-portfolio-refresh-race). New IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService runs a bulk DELETE in a fresh scope, invoked (a) at the tail of PortfolioUpdater.Update so cleanup cadence = refresh cadence, and (b) once at host startup. Race-free because: fresh scope (no ChangeTracker bleed), ExecuteDeleteAsync (no SaveChanges interception), queue serialisation (no concurrent cleanup), DB-level FK cascade for FeatureWork/Forecasts/SimulationResults/Portfolios/Deliveries.

validation.method: Inline by orchestrator — design.md captures three-option comparison and chosen approach; user approved option C + /nw-deliver directly; cascade FK behaviour verified against InitialCreate.cs migration; solution-architect dispatch skipped per /nw-deliver-direct convention.

phases.name: Orphaned Feature cleanup service + integration
phases.description: Add the IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService with bulk-DELETE implementation, wire it into PortfolioUpdater's tail, and add a one-shot startup invocation.

steps.name: Add IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService + impl + unit tests
steps.description: Create the interface and a single implementation that opens a fresh IServiceScope, resolves a fresh LighthouseAppContext, and issues a bulk `ExecuteDeleteAsync` for Features where `!IsParentFeature && !Portfolios.Any()`. Write unit tests covering: (a) orphan with no Portfolios is deleted, (b) Feature linked to a Portfolio is preserved, (c) parent Feature with empty Portfolios is preserved, (d) cascade behaviour deletes dependent FeatureWork/Forecast rows.
criteria: NEW file `Lighthouse.Backend/Lighthouse.Backend/Services/Interfaces/IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService.cs` with single method `Task<int> CleanupAsync(CancellationToken cancellationToken = default)` returning the deleted count.

NEW file `Lighthouse.Backend/Lighthouse.Backend/Services/Implementation/OrphanedFeatureCleanupService.cs` implementing the interface. Constructor takes `IServiceScopeFactory scopeFactory` + `ILogger<OrphanedFeatureCleanupService> logger`. Body: `using var scope = scopeFactory.CreateScope(); var db = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<LighthouseAppContext>(); var deleted = await db.Features.Where(f => !f.IsParentFeature && !f.Portfolios.Any()).ExecuteDeleteAsync(cancellationToken); if (deleted > 0) { logger.LogInformation('Cleaned up {Count} orphaned features', deleted); } return deleted;`

NEW test file `Lighthouse.Backend/Lighthouse.Backend.Tests/Services/Implementation/OrphanedFeatureCleanupServiceTests.cs` with 4 tests covering the acceptance criteria points. Use SQLite in-memory (`UseSqlite('DataSource=:memory:')`) so `ExecuteDeleteAsync` actually executes — EF InMemory provider has limited support for raw delete SQL. Seed data via direct DbSet.Add + SaveChanges, then invoke `CleanupAsync` and assert against the DB state.
Tests follow project conventions: NUnit [TestFixture], factory-style data via local helpers, no shared mutable state across tests beyond what [SetUp] provides, no comments inside tests.
Service is NOT yet wired into DI or called from PortfolioUpdater — step 01-02 handles that.
All 4 new tests pass; full backend suite (excluding external connector categories) still green.
steps.scenario_name: OrphanedFeatureCleanupService deletes Features with no Portfolio refs, preserves linked + parent Features, cascades dependents
quality_gates: cd Lighthouse.Backend && dotnet build /warnaserror — zero warnings
cd Lighthouse.Backend && dotnet test --filter 'FullyQualifiedName~OrphanedFeatureCleanupService' — 4 tests pass
cd Lighthouse.Backend && dotnet test --filter 'Category!=JiraIntegration&Category!=AdoIntegration&Category!=LinearIntegration' — full non-external suite still green (2323 + 4 = 2327 tests)
steps.implementation_notes: Use SQLite in-memory connection that stays open for the duration of the test fixture (`var connection = new SqliteConnection('DataSource=:memory:'); connection.Open(); var options = new DbContextOptionsBuilder<LighthouseAppContext>().UseSqlite(connection).Options;`). Call `db.Database.EnsureCreated()` to create the schema. The Feature constructor signature is `new Feature(Team team, int defaultWorkItems)`. Order is NOT NULL — set `Order = '1'` on every constructed Feature in tests. To create a linked Feature, add the Portfolio to `feature.Portfolios` (List<Portfolio>) AND the Feature to `portfolio.Features` — relationship fixup handles the join. SaveChanges before invoking CleanupAsync to commit to DB.

steps.name: Wire IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService into PortfolioUpdater tail + Program.cs startup
steps.description: Register the service in DI. Invoke it at the tail of PortfolioUpdater.Update (after the existing try/finally, so cleanup failures don't affect refresh state). Invoke it once during host startup (in Program.cs, before app.Run() or via an `IHostedService` that runs once on start). Update PortfolioUpdaterTest to verify cleanup is called.
criteria: `Lighthouse.Backend/Lighthouse.Backend/Program.cs`: register `builder.Services.AddSingleton<IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService, OrphanedFeatureCleanupService>();` next to the other Services registrations.

`Lighthouse.Backend/Lighthouse.Backend/Program.cs`: after `var app = builder.Build();` and before `app.Run();`, invoke cleanup once: resolve `IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService` from `app.Services` and call `await CleanupAsync()`. Log the count via `app.Logger`. Wrap in try/catch — startup cleanup failure must not block boot.

`Lighthouse.Backend/Lighthouse.Backend/Services/Implementation/BackgroundServices/Update/PortfolioUpdater.cs`: inject `IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService` via constructor. At the END of `Update(int id, IServiceProvider serviceProvider)`, after the try/finally block, call `await cleanupService.CleanupAsync();`. Wrap in try/catch and log on failure — cleanup failure must not affect refresh outcome (the refresh has already been logged in the finally).

`Lighthouse.Backend/Lighthouse.Backend.Tests/Services/Implementation/BackgroundServices/Update/PortfolioUpdaterTest.cs`: add a Mock<IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService> to the test fixture, pass it through the constructor, and verify `CleanupAsync` is called exactly once per Update invocation. The mock returns 0 by default.
All existing PortfolioUpdaterTest tests continue to pass after the constructor signature change.
Build clean, full backend suite green, no DeliveriesControllerIntegrationTest regression.
steps.scenario_name: PortfolioUpdater invokes IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService.CleanupAsync after each Update; startup cleanup runs once during host build-up
quality_gates: cd Lighthouse.Backend && dotnet build /warnaserror — zero warnings
cd Lighthouse.Backend && dotnet test --filter 'FullyQualifiedName~PortfolioUpdaterTest' — all pass
cd Lighthouse.Backend && dotnet test --filter 'Category!=JiraIntegration&Category!=AdoIntegration&Category!=LinearIntegration' — full non-external suite green
steps.implementation_notes: PortfolioUpdater currently has a primary-constructor signature inheriting from UpdateServiceBase<Portfolio>. Adding a new constructor param requires updating both the primary constructor declaration AND PortfolioUpdaterTest's instantiation (look for `new PortfolioUpdater(...)` or DI resolution). The Mock for IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService should be passed last in the primary-constructor argument order. The startup invocation in Program.cs should resolve via `using var scope = app.Services.CreateScope(); var cleanup = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<IOrphanedFeatureCleanupService>(); await cleanup.CleanupAsync();` to avoid lifetime-mismatch issues with the singleton service taking IServiceScopeFactory.
